{{Short description|American psychologist}}
{{Use American English|date=March 2026}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=March 2026}}
'''Hal Pashler''' is Distinguished Professor of Psychology at [[University of California, San Diego]].  An experimental psychologist and cognitive scientist, Pashler is best known for his studies of human attentional limitations (his analysis of the [[Psychological refractory period]] effect concluded that the brain has discrete "processing bottlenecks" associated with specific types of cognitive operations).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pashler |first1=H. |date=September 1994 |title=Dual-task interference in simple tasks: data and theory. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=116 |issue=2 |pages=220–44 |citeseerx=10.1.1.324.4916 |pmid=7972591|doi=10.1037/0033-2909.116.2.220 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last=Zimmer |first=C. |date=15 November 2010 |title=The Brain: The "Router" in Your Head—a Bottleneck of Processing |url=http://discovermagazine.com/2010/nov/15-the-brain-router-in-our-heads-processing-bottleneck |work=Discover Magazine}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Bush |first1=C. |date=8 April 2001 |title=HOW TO; Multitask |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2001/04/08/magazine/how-to-multitask.html |work=The New York Times}}</ref> and for his work on visual attention<ref>{{cite journal |last=Moss |first=S. |date=1999 |title=How to saw the Concept of Attention in Half without Sacrificing the Subject: Review of the Psychology of Attention by Harold Pashler |url=http://journalpsyche.org/files/0xaa5d.pdf |journal=Psyche: An Interdisciplinary Journal of Research on Consciousness |volume=5 |issue=13 |citeseerx=10.1.1.518.665}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Huang |first1=L. |last2=Pashler |first2=H. |title=A Boolean map theory of visual attention. |url=http://www.pashler.com/Articles/Huang_Pashler_PR2007.pdf |journal=Psychological Review |date=2007 |volume=114 |issue=3 |pages=599–631 |doi=10.1037/0033-295X.114.3.599 |pmid=17638498}}</ref> He has also developed and tested new methods for enhancing learning and reducing forgetting, focusing on the temporal spacing of learning and retrieval practice.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rohrer |first1=D. |last2=Pashler |first2=H. |date=2010 |title=Recent Research on Human Learning Challenges Conventional Instructional Strategies |journal=Educational Researcher |volume=39 |issue=5 |pages=406–412 |doi=10.3102/0013189X10374770|s2cid=2982177 }}</ref>

Pashler is also known for influential critiques of methodological and statistical practices in behavioral science.  His critiques have focused on statistical and logical issues in neuroimaging research ("voodoo correlations"),<ref name=Begley>{{cite web |last=Begley |first=S. |date=20 January 2009 |title=Of Voodoo and the Brain |url=http://www.newsweek.com/id/182570 |work=Newsweek}}</ref> [[educational psychology]] ([[learning styles]] concept)<ref>{{cite web |last=Cosh |first=C. |date=6 January 2010 |title=If you're an aural learner, read this aloud to yourself |url=http://www.macleans.ca/uncategorized/if-youre-an-aural-learner-read-this-aloud-to-yourself/ |work=Maclean's Magazine}}</ref>  testing of mathematical models,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Roberts |first1=S. |last2=Pashler |first2=H. |date=2000 |title=How persuasive is a good fit? A comment on theory testing. |journal=Psychological Review |volume=107 |issue=2 |pages=358–67 |pmid=10789200|doi=10.1037/0033-295X.107.2.358 |s2cid=14741205 |url=http://www.escholarship.org/uc/item/5vt0z72k }}</ref> and the replicability of “behavioral priming” research in the field of social psychology.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bower |first1=B. |date=19 May 2012 |title=The hot and cold of priming: Psychologists are divided on whether unnoticed cues can influence behavior |journal=Science News |volume=181 |issue=10 |pages=26–29 |doi=10.1002/scin.5591811025}}</ref>

==Education==
Pashler was born in 1958 in New York.  He received his BA in Logic and [[Philosophy of science|Philosophy of Science]] from [[Brown University]] in 1980 and his PhD in psychology from the [[University of Pennsylvania]] in 1985. He joined the faculty of the [[University of California, San Diego]] in 1985.

==Honors==
Pashler was elected to membership in the [[Society of Experimental Psychologists]] in 2000. He is also an elected Fellow of the [[Association for Psychological Science]] and the [[Psychonomic Society]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Fellows |url=http://www.sepsych.org/fellows.php |publisher=The Society of Experimental Psychologists |access-date=2016-11-30 |archive-date=2013-06-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130614110114/http://www.sepsych.org/fellows.php |url-status=dead }}</ref>
Pashler received the [[Troland Research Awards]] from the [[National Academy of Sciences]] in 1999; the academy cited his "many experimental breakthroughs in the study of spatial attention and executive control, and... his insightful analysis of human [[cognitive architecture]]." He also received the Chancellor's Associates Award for Research given by [[University of California, San Diego]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Chancellor's Associates Faculty Excellence Award Winners: 2005–06 |url=https://academicaffairs.ucsd.edu/faculty/awards/chancellors-associates/recipients.html#2005-06 |publisher=UC San Diego}}</ref>

==Research career==

In the 1980s, Pashler and several colleagues developed the Response Selection Bottleneck model of dual-task interference. The model, partly inspired by early work by Alan Welford,<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Welford |first=A. T. |date=1952 |title=The 'psychological Refractory Period' and the Timing of High-Speed Performance—a Review and a Theory |journal=British Journal of Psychology |volume=43 |issue=1 |pages=2–19 |doi=10.1111/j.2044-8295.1952.tb00322.x}}</ref> makes many predictions about patterns of behavioral response times in the [[Psychological refractory period|Psychological Refractory Period]] experiment. The model has been supported by mathematical analyses of behavioral response times<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sigman |first1=M. |last2=Dehaene |first2=S. |date=2005 |title=Parsing a Cognitive Task: A Characterization of the Mind's Bottleneck |journal=PLOS Biology |volume=3 |issue=2 |pages=e37 |doi=10.1371/journal.pbio.0030037 |pmc=546328 |pmid=15719056 |doi-access=free }}</ref> and studies of brain activity when people engage in multitasking.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Marti |first1=S. |last2=Sigman |first2=M. |last3=Dehaene |first3=S. |date=2012 |title=A shared cortical bottleneck underlying Attentional Blink and Psychological Refractory Period |url=https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-00697406 |journal=NeuroImage |volume=59 |issue=3 |pages=2883–98 |doi=10.1016/j.neuroimage.2011.09.063 |pmid=21988891|hdl=11336/56976 |citeseerx=10.1.1.397.4239 |s2cid=15024677 }}</ref>

[[File:Response Selection Bottleneck.png|thumb|left|Response Selection Bottleneck Model of Dual-Task Interference]]

In 1988, Pashler published the first demonstration of the perceptual phenomenon that later came to be called [[change blindness]], using displays of letters that appeared, disappeared, and reappeared (sometimes with alterations).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pashler |first1=H. |year=1988 |title=Familiarity and visual change detection |journal=Perception & Psychophysics |volume=44 |issue=4 |pages=369–378 |doi=10.3758/BF03210419 |pmid=3226885 |s2cid=17545490 |doi-access=free }}</ref> He noted the contrast between observers' subjective sense of awareness of an entire display and their very limited ability to detect even large changes.

In 1992, Pashler (with Mark Carrier) showed that the [[testing effect]] (sometimes referred to as Retrieval Practice) directly strengthens associative learning, and does so more effectively than the same time spent re-studying the same associative links.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Carrier |first1=M. |last2=Pashler |first2=H. |year=1992 |title=The influence of retrieval on retention |url=http://dualtask2.org/publication_visit.php?url=articles/Carrier_Pashler_MemCog1992.pdf |journal=Memory & Cognition |volume=20 |issue=6 |pages=633–642 |doi=10.3758/BF03202713 |pmid=1435266|s2cid=15893469 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
In 2007, Liqiang Huang and Pashler proposed the Boolean Map Theory of visual attention and awareness.&nbsp;The theory argues that a specific type of abstract data structure (the Boolean Map) characterizes the contents of human visual awareness at any given instant in time.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Huang |first1=L. |last2=Pashler |first2=H. |year=2007 |title=A Boolean map theory of visual attention |journal=Psychological Review |volume=114 |issue=3 |pages=599–631 |doi=10.1037/0033-295x.114.3.599 |pmid=17638498}}</ref>

[[File:Boolean Map Theory.png|thumb|right|Boolean Map Theory of Attention to Multidimensional Stimuli]]

In 2008 (with Melody Wiseheart & other collaborators) Pashler carried out the most systematic and long-term studies of the effect of temporal spacing on human learning. Holding total time constant, the team found that when people study information on two occasions separated by a temporal gap G, and then are given a memory test after a further delay D, performance on the test is best when the G is about 10-20% as long as D.

<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cepeda |first1=N. J. |last2=Vul |first2=E. |last3=Rohrer |first3=D. |last4=Wixted |first4=J. T. |last5=Pashler |first5=H. |year=2008 |title=Spacing effects in learning: a temporal ridgeline of optimal retention |url=http://laplab.ucsd.edu/articles/Cepeda_et_al_2008_psychsci.pdf |journal=Psychological Science |volume=19 |issue=11 |pages=1095–102 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02209.x |pmid=19076480 |s2cid=3163864 }}{{Dead link|date=January 2020 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> [[File:Joint_Effects_of_Inter-Study_Gap_and_Test_Delay_on_Human_Memory.png|thumb|left|Human Memory as a Joint Function of Inter-Study Gap and Test Delay as revealed in data from Cepeda, Pashler, & Colleagues]]  (This implies, for example, that if one wishes to study information on two occasions with the goal of retaining it for 1 year, the best practice would be to separate the two study events by about 1 month, whereas if one wished to retain the information for a week, a 1-day gap would work better.) This research is relied upon by some software developers in building spaced-learning apps and websites to help people to retain information more efficiently<ref>
[http://www.ollielovell.com/olliesclassroom/help-students-remember/s] {{dead link|date=July 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.mytools2learn.com/education/spaced-learning |title=Courses - MyTools2Learn |access-date=2016-11-30 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161024005500/http://www.mytools2learn.com/education/spaced-learning |archive-date=2016-10-24 }}</ref>

In 2006, US Department of Education commissioned Pashler to lead a review of cognitive science findings that could best help guide teachers in scientifically validated instructional practices. The resulting Practice Guide is distributed by the department and is available free of charge on the department's website.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Pashler |first1=H. |last2=Bain |first2=P. M. |last3=Bottge |first3=B. A. |last4=Graesser |first4=A. |last5=Koedinger |first5=K. |last6=McDaniel |first6=M. |last7=Metcalfe |first7= J. |year=2007 |title=Organizing Instruction and Study to Improve Student Learning: A Practice Guide |url=https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/Docs/PracticeGuide/20072004.pdf |publisher=National Center for Education Research, Institute of Education Sciences, US Department of Education}}</ref>

In 2008 (with Edward Vul) Pashler showed that the famous [[The Wisdom of Crowds|Wisdom of Crowds]] Effect first noted by [[Francis Galton]] could be elicited within a single individual. Averaging two estimates from the same person (their first-choice answer and a second answer elicited later) produced an improvement in accuracy equal to about 1/3 the benefit obtained by averaging estimates from two different people.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vul |first1=E. |last2=Pashler |first2=H. |year=2008 |title=Measuring the Crowd Within |url=http://escholarship.org/uc/item/7x1799rm#page-1 |journal=Psychological Science |volume=19 |issue=7 |pages=645–647 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02136.x|pmid=18727777 |citeseerx=10.1.1.408.4760 |s2cid=44718192 }}</ref> This discovery, termed the “Inner Crowd Effect” by ''[[The Economist]]''<ref>{{cite news |date=26 June 2008 |title=The crowd within |url=https://www.economist.com/science-and-technology/2008/06/26/the-crowd-within |newspaper=The Economist }}</ref> helped prompt follow-up research examining potential new methods of improving human judgment accuracy.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Soll |first1=J. B. |last2=Larrick |first2=R. P. |year=2009 |title=Strategies for revising judgment: how (and how well) people use others' opinions |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition |volume=35 |issue=3 |pages=780–805 |citeseerx=10.1.1.153.8244 |doi=10.1037/a0015145 |pmid=19379049}}</ref>
Pashler has also published several articles on varied topics in political psychology, including biases in perception of newsworthiness and attitudes to pro-liberty views.<ref>Pashler, Hal: “U.S. Immigrants’ Attitudes Toward Libertarian Values” (2013). Department of Psychology, University of California, San Diego. [https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2234200]</ref>

==Controversies==
In 2008, Pashler (along with Vul, Harris, and Winkielman) published a paper initially entitled “Voodoo Correlations in Social Neuroscience” arguing that many of the most prominent research articles in cognitive and social neuroscience had made statistical errors resulting in gross over-estimation of brain-behavior correlation values.  The paper (published under the milder title "Puzzlingly Large Correlations...")<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vul |first1=E. |last2=Harris |first2=C. |last3=Winkielman |first3=P. |last4=Pashler |first4=H. |date=2009 |title=Puzzlingly High Correlations in fMRI Studies of Emotion, Personality, and Social Cognition. |url=http://dualtask2.org/publication_visit.php?url=articles/Vul_etal_2008inpress.pdf |journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science |volume=4 |issue=3 |pages=274–90 |doi=10.1111/j.1745-6924.2009.01125.x |pmid=26158964|s2cid=242553 }}</ref> produced an intense controversy that was covered in popular media<ref name=Begley/> as well as academic press.<ref>{{cite web |last=Giles |first=J. |date=14 January 2009 |title=Doubts raised over brain scan findings |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/mg20126914-700-doubts-raised-over-brain-scan-findings/ |website=New Scientist}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Abbott |first1=A. |year=2009 |title=Brain imaging studies under fire |journal=Nature |volume=457 |issue=7227 |pages=245 |doi=10.1038/457245a|pmid=19148065 |s2cid=10076461 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Some writers have pointed to this paper as having helped launch the current period of intensive methodological debate and controversy in behavioral science<ref>{{cite web |last=Gelman |first=A. |date=21 September 2016 |title=What has happened down here is the winds have changed |url=http://andrewgelman.com/2016/09/21/what-has-happened-down-here-is-the-winds-have-changed/ |website=Statistical Modeling, Causal Inference, and Social Science}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Voodoo Correlations: Two Years Later |url=http://neurocritic.blogspot.ca/2010/12/voodoo-correlations-two-years-later.html |website=The Neurocritic}}</ref> Statistical practices in brain imaging field appear to have changed in response to the paper<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vul |first1=E. |last2=Pashler |first2=H. |date=2012 |title=Voodoo and circularity errors |url=http://laplab.ucsd.edu/articles/Vul_Pashler2012.pdf |journal=NeuroImage |volume=62 |issue=2 |pages=945–948 |doi=10.1016/j.neuroimage.2012.01.027|pmid=22270348 |s2cid=18230380 }}</ref> with increased use of [[Cross-validation (statistics)|cross-validation]].

In 2009, Pashler chaired a review commissioned by the American Psychological Society examining the validity of the concept of [[learning styles]]<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pashler |first1=H. |last2=McDaniel |first2=M. |last3=Rohrer |first3=D. |last4=Bjork |first4=R. |title=Learning Styles |journal=Psychological Science in the Public Interest |date=December 2009 |volume=9 |issue=3 |pages=105–119 |doi=10.1111/j.1539-6053.2009.01038.x |pmid=26162104 |s2cid=2112166 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The review concluded that widely accepted ideas about learning styles lacked serious empirical support and recommended that educators should not base instructional practices on these notions. These conclusions have prompted extensive controversy in the education field.<ref>{{cite web  |last=Ferro |first=S. |date=12 September 2013  |title=Everything you've ever been told about how you learn is a lie  |url=http://www.popsci.com/science/article/2013-08/everything-youve-ever-been-told-about-how-brain-learns-lie |work=Popular Science}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last=Guteri |first=S. |date=20 September 2013 |title=Is teaching to student's learning stye a bogus idea |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/is-teaching-to-a-students-learning-style-a-bogus-idea/ |work=Scientific American }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last=Glenn |first=D. |date=15 December 2009 |title=Matching teaching style to learning style may not help students |url=http://chronicle.com/article/Matching-Teaching-Style-to/49497/?key=STp2dlw5aHMdNCZjfCcRfStXYHt9cUl%2FbSJCYSgaYFxT  |newspaper=Chronicle of Higher Education }}</ref> Pashler has also been an outspoken proponent of efforts to increase replicability in psychological research, arguing that many very well-known findings in areas like [[social cognition]] and social psychology should not be believed due to statistical errors, [[publication bias]], and other problems.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Abbott |first1=A. |date=2013 |title=Disputed results a fresh blow for social psychology |journal=Nature |volume=497 |issue=7447 |pages=16 |doi=10.1038/497016a|pmid=23636371 |bibcode=2013Natur.497...16A |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal  |last1=Bower |first1=B. |year=2012 |title=The hot and cold of priming: Psychologists are divided on whether unnoticed cues can influence behavior |journal=Science News |volume=181 |issue=10 |pages=26–29 |doi=10.1002/scin.5591811025}}</ref>  Together with E. J. Wagenmakers, he edited the special issue of the ''Perspectives on Psychological Science'' which appears to have introduced the term ‘Replicability Crisis’ in reference to the current state of social and behavioral science.<ref>{{cite journal  |title=Special Section on Replicability in Psychological Science: A Crisis of Confidence? |journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science  |volume=7 |issue=6 |url=http://pps.sagepub.com/content/7/6.toc}}</ref>
